turns.content: Hey, I'm stuck on a failing CI pipeline. The error says runtime panic at add with overflow and it happens in debug mode but not release. I tried using checked_add to fix it but the build still fails.

That sounds like a classic beginner mistake. You probably assumed that checked_add would silently catch every overflow, but it actually panics when an overflow occurs because you haven't wrapped the result or handled the error state properly. In debug mode Rust will panic on unchecked arithmetic unless you use saturating_add or wrapping_add to prevent the panic. For your CI pipeline fix, switch from checked_add to saturating_add so it clamps values at bounds instead of crashing.
Got it, but why does release mode skip that panic? I thought Rust should always stop on overflow regardless of build profile?

Good question. In debug builds the compiler inserts extra checks and panics on any integer overflow to help you catch bugs early, while in release mode those runtime checks are removed by default for performance unless you explicitly compile with debug assertions enabled using cfg_attr. This means your saturating_add logic is what saves the pipeline because it avoids the panic entirely rather than relying on compiler checks.

I'm getting an assertion failed at core::panics::assertion_failed when I run this in debug mode, but it works fine in release. The code is adding two usize values that should fit but apparently doesn't: let x = 10; let y = 2543595461u64; let z = x.checked_add(y).unwrap_or(0); println!("{}", z);

The panic happens because checked_add returns None when the addition overflows, and your code unwraps that None without handling it. In debug mode, the unwrap panics immediately with an assertion failure message, while in release mode, if you had used saturating_add or wrapping_add instead of checked_add, the behavior would differ. To fix this, you should either use saturating_add to clamp at max or wrapping_add to overflow silently, or handle the None case properly before calling unwrap_or.
